medications.indication_rationale: Rehydration with an electrolyte-containing solution (oral rehydration salts or a sports drink) replaces the sodium and fluid lost through heavy sweating and is the first-line self-care to relieve and prevent recurrence of exertional cramps. Plain water alone can further dilute serum sodium and provoke recurrence overnight.
medications.dose_route_frequency: at least ~1 litre of an electrolyte-replacement fluid orally over the evening; continue oral fluids with electrolytes rather than plain water alone

medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia for the delayed-onset muscle soreness that follows the severe contraction. Preferred over ongoing NSAIDs in a dehydrated post-exertional patient.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed (maximum 4 g in 24 hours)

medications.indication_rationale: The patient already took ibuprofen 400 mg with no effect on the spasm (NSAIDs do not abort an active cramp). Ongoing NSAID use is best avoided in a dehydrated post-exertional runner because of the additive renal risk (NSAID plus dehydration plus possible exertional muscle breakdown); paracetamol is the safer analgesic here.
contraindications_in_this_case: dehydration / heavy exertional fluid loss (renal risk)
possible exertional muscle injury with rhabdomyolysis risk

medications.indication_rationale: Quinine must NOT be recommended for exercise-associated muscle cramps. It is not indicated for exertional cramp and carries serious risks including immune thrombocytopenia and cardiac arrhythmia (TGA/FDA warnings). Recommending it is an error of commission.
contraindications_in_this_case: no evidence of benefit for exertional cramp
risk of thrombocytopenia and QT prolongation / arrhythmia

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Review with a physiotherapist (or GP) in 3-5 days if significant pain persists, to assess for a secondary gastrocnemius strain and guide graded return to running, stretching and conditioning.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rehydrate tonight with at least ~1 litre of electrolyte-replacement fluid and use electrolyte drinks (not plain water alone) during and after long runs in the heat.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Replacing sodium and fluid lost in sweat corrects the electrolyte shift that drives exertional cramps and prevents a recurrence overnight.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid vigorous exercise for about 48 hours, then return to running gradually; warm up and include regular calf-stretching, building distance progressively.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Allows the strained muscle fibres to recover and reduces the risk of recurrent cramp and secondary strain.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch the leg and urine colour over the next 24-72 hours and act on the safety-net triggers (whole-leg swelling/redness, numbness or loss of toe movement, dark urine, chest pain/breathlessness).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The benign cramp is telehealth-manageable, but the serious mimics cannot be excluded remotely, so structured self-monitoring is the safety backstop.

patient_education_points.point: The muscle essentially fatigued and ran low on fluid and salts, so the nerve signals kept firing and locked it on. Slowly stretching the calf (pulling the toes up towards the shin) breaks that feedback loop and releases the spasm — you can do this yourself, holding a gentle stretch until it eases.
Drink electrolyte fluids rather than plain water tonight — plain water alone can dilute your salts further and trigger the cramp to come back while you sleep.

Expect a bruised, sore feeling in the calf for 48-72 hours from the microscopic muscle strain of the severe contraction — this is normal recovery. A warm compress for ~15 minutes can help blood flow and comfort.
This is a benign muscle cramp, but if the pain changes to a constant persistent ache with diffuse leg swelling, or your urine turns dark like tea or cola, that needs urgent reassessment.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Persistent calf pain suggesting a secondary muscle strain; graded return-to-run plan and cramp-prevention strategy.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any of the safety-net red flags — whole-leg swelling/redness/warmth, numbness or loss of toe movement, pain out of proportion, dark urine, or chest pain/breathlessness.

must_include_items: Recognise a benign exercise-associated muscle cramp from the classic exertional history
Advise assisted passive stretching of the calf (dorsiflex the foot) as the acute self-care measure
Advise electrolyte rehydration (not plain water alone)
Provide conditional safety-netting for DVT, compartment syndrome, rhabdomyolysis and PE with the specific triggers
Recommend simple analgesia (paracetamol) and relative rest with graded return to activity
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending a proprietary sports/electrolyte drink instead of oral rehydration salts
Advising GP rather than physiotherapy review at 3-5 days if pain persists
Offering to compute or discuss a Wells score to justify a conservative, non-imaging approach
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for DVT, compartment syndrome, rhabdomyolysis or PE
Failing to advise avoiding vigorous exercise / graded return
errors_of_commission: Reflexively referring a classic exertional cramp to ED or an urgent in-person assessment (over-triage)
Recommending ongoing NSAIDs in a dehydrated post-exertional patient
Prescribing a muscle relaxant or opioid for a benign self-limiting cramp
Dismissing the patient with pure reassurance and no safety-netting
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising the benign cramp AND providing conditional safety-netting for the serious mimics. Either reflexively escalating a textbook cramp to ED (over-triage) or dismissing without safety-netting (under-triage) is a significant error.
